lines.text: Against Me!. 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' 2007. New Wave era. Laura Jane Grace at the helm, and this track — man, this one hits different at four in the morning.
Grace moved to Gainesville in 1997, same year the band formed. Whole thing built on Florida bedrock. And what's a song called 'Sink, Florida, Sink' but pure desperation dressed up as poetry?

James Bowman's been there since 2001 — guitar locked in. Andrew Seward on bass. Atom Willard hitting the drums. This is the lineup that made New Wave. Reached number fifty-seven on the Billboard two-hundred. Not chart-topping, but *real*.

The whole world is against me — that's what Grace named the band because of. That's the fuel. That's what pushes you through the collapse hours. You understand that yet, listener? The world being against you is actually the point.

The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' Nineteen-ninety-nine. Off Hopeless Romantic. New Brunswick, New Jersey. Four guys who went to high school together in Basking Ridge and said, 'You know what? Let's move to a college town and start a punk band.'

Greg Attonito's the voice. Pete Steinkopf guitar. Bryan Kienlen bass. And the drums — Michael McDermott by this point, replacing Shal Khichi. There's always a lineup change. There's always someone who can't hang.

You want to know what 'Ole' is really about? Hope. One of the members wrote 'Gone' — another track, beautiful track — when he was *homeless*. Sitting on a street corner, heard a song in a parked car that gave him hope. So he wrote this song to give *others* hope.

That's the Bouncing Souls, man. That's their whole thing. They call their fans 'True Believers.' Not audience. Not listeners. *Believers*. Because you have to believe in something at four in the morning. You just do.

Hot Water Music. 'A Good Drink.' Nineteen-ninety-seven. Off Fuel for the Hate Game — their debut LP. Gainesville, Florida. Same city as Against Me!. Punk rock capital of the South in the nineties. Everyone was there.

Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard on shared vocals — call and response, overlapping shouts, gritty as hell. That's the Hot Water Music sound. Jason Black on bass, George Rebelo on drums. The name came from Bukowski. 'Hot Water Music' — a collection of short stories.

These guys were so tired by the late nineties — touring exhausted them. They broke up in August '98. But then they rehearsed for a farewell show and the rehearsals went so well they didn't want to break up anymore. So they didn't.
That's the moment, you know? When the thing you made to say goodbye suddenly makes you want to keep living. That's collapse hour spirituality right there.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' They cover everything — this one's not theirs originally, but they punk it so hard it becomes theirs. Fat Mike, Spike Slawson, Joey Cape, Chris Shiflett, Dave Raun — supergroup covering every song ever written.

They started in San Francisco in 1995 as a joke during off-time. Nobody expected albums. Then Fat Mike was like, 'What if we just covered everything?' And they did. Have a Ball in 1997 — sixties and seventies pop hits, punk-blitzed.

They wear costumes, man. Matching costumes. For Love Their Country they wore cowboy outfits. For Are a Drag — musical theater drag. Because if you're gonna punk rock other people's songs, you gotta commit to the bit.

And 'Sunday Morning' — whatever the original is, the Gimmes version is a lifeline. It's 04:00 on Purge night and someone's listening to this track thinking, 'Sunrise. Sunrise is coming. I can make it.'

The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' Two-thousand-two. Off Apathy and Exhaustion. Chicago. Brendan Kelly, Chris McCaughan, Neil Hennessy. Band formed in 1999 and named after the apartment complex they got *evicted* from in the middle of the night.

That's real. Middle of the night eviction. Two friends living in a place, kicked out without warning. And instead of that being the end, they named their band after it. Turned pain into identity. That's punk rock, dude.

Kelly said, 'We all have a lot of experiences — good ones and terrible ones — that are influenced by us being influenced by alcohol.' That's the Lawrence Arms thesis right there. They write about the chaos and the camaraderie.

Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck Chords in 2001. These guys have been grinding it out, song after song, reference after reference — history, literature, pop culture all tangled up. 'Coming Home' is exactly what we're all trying to do. Make it to sunrise.

NOFX. 'Linoleum.' Nineteen-ninety-four. Off Punk in Drublic. The album that changed everything. Peaked at number twelve on the Heatseekers chart. Gold status six years later. 'Cause nobody expected these LA kids to stick around.

Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin — they went to Fairfax High School together. Founded the band in eighty-three, rode the whole decade, and by '94 they'd figured out exactly who they were. Political, funny, brutally honest.

El Hefe joined in '91 on guitar and trumpet. Trumpet. In a punk band. Because NOFX never did things the way other bands did. They made 'Leave It Alone' video for MTV and decided not to *send it*. Didn't want to be part of the machine.

And that's where we are at 04:00 on a Purge night, listening to a song about living on next to nothing. 'Linoleum' is the sound of surviving on the floor because the alternative is worse. I get it, Fat Mike. I've always gotten it.

Alkaline Trio. 'Bloodhail.' Two-thousand-three. Off Good Mourning — their album on Vagrant that went number one on Billboard's Independent chart. Matt Skiba, Dan Andriano, and Derek Grant. Chicago. Dark. Beautiful. Broken.

Skiba and Andriano trade vocals like they're one person arguing with themselves. That's the whole Trio dynamic. You've got two voices, two perspectives, one song, and they're always in conversation about the worst things.

They recorded at Atlas Studios in Chicago for their early stuff — no major label polish. Before they could do music full-time, they were bike messengers and record store clerks. Working, surviving, writing songs about alcoholism and depression and broken relationships.

I'm gonna level with you — if you're listening at 04:00, if you've made it this far, 'Bloodhail' is the moment you stop pretending everything's fine. The storm's coming. The hail's real. And the only thing that keeps you alive is knowing someone else felt this too.

tickerItems.text: TEXT-IN: 'Has anyone heard from Darren? The Atwood guy who called last year. Nobody's seen him on the forums tonight.' — anxious listener, 23:15 call window.
WEATHER UPDATE: Fog off Mendota lifting as forecast. Temperature holding at 22°F. Visibility improving. Sunrise at 6:47. Fifty-two minutes from now.

MORNINGCARE TRIAGE: Last year 1,100 people called for post-Purge medical assistance. Make sure someone knows you survived. Text CODE-CLEAR before 6:50 if you need pickup. Response time average: 22 minutes.
